Eduard Serra

Mechanisms of cellular self-renewal and differentiation, peptide aptamer development

serra@molsci.org
510-981-8726 (w)

Roles
Research Research@MSI
Research The Alpha Project

Biography
Education
B.S. in Biology, University of Barcelona, 1995
Ph.D. in Biology, University of Barcelona, 2001

Ph.D. thesis title, "Genetic, cellular and molecular studies of neurofibromas"

Joined the Institute in October 2001

Graduate Research

Previous work studying the role of the NF1 gene in the development of neurofibromas (benign peripheral nervous system tumors) and the characterization of a mutational somatic spectrum for this gene in these heterogeneous tumors. Also involved the demonstration of Schwann cells as the specific cell type that initiate neurofibroma formation and the study of mitotic recombination as a mutational genetic mechanisms in this cell type.

Current Research

Mammalian cell genetics

His current research interest is the use of peptide aptamers as a tool for performing forward and reverse genetics in mammalian cells. Involved in Designing Improved Peptide Aptamer Libraries, with Ian Burbulis and Richard Yu. In particular, he is interested in applying this technology for studying embryonic stem cells, focussing on genetic pathways that govern self-renewal and specific cell fate differentiation.

Alpha project

Also collaborating on the Alpha Project. He is interested in signal transduction pathway specificity and crosstalk; in the reciprocal interaction between the cell cycle machinery and signal transduction pathways that trigger differentiation; in the molecular mechanisms that regulate cell size; and in cell-to-cell and strain-to-strain variability. He is collaborating in the fine characterization of cellular response to different doses of alpha factor, both qualitatively and quantitatively, and in the development of single cell measurement technologies for the latter.

Publications

Ars, E, Kruyer, H, Gaona, A, Serra, E, L?zaro, C, Estivill, X. Prenatal diagnosis of sporadic neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) by RNA and DNA analysis of a splicing mutation. Prenat Diagn 1999, 19, 739-742 PubMed
Ars, E, Kruyer, H, Gaona, A, Casquero, P, Rosell, J, Volpini, V, Serra, E, L?zaro, C, Estivill, X. A clinical variant of neurofibromatosis type 1: familial spinal neurofibromatosis with a frameshift mutation in the NF1 gene. Am J Hum Genet 1998, 62, 834-841 PubMed
